# Data information
Input data:
- Complete genome assembly: obtain from Genbank (contains chromosome and plasmids)
- Shovill simulated: only the chromosome 
- The realfastq simulated: only the chromosome
- .mmi the genome pathway
- .gfa is the assembly graph

# Running Eris
We investigate the output file of simulated and complete genome assembly. 

::: {#6761721d .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# # Run Eris with simulated data
# eris -i data/contigs_shovill_simulated.gfa \
#      -d data/IS.mmi \
#      -o output/eris_output_simulated


# # Run Eris for only the contigs of the complete genome assembly
# eris -i data/contigs_complete_genome_assembly.gfa -d data/IS.mmi -o output/eris_output_complete_genome
```
:::


Have error from previous code and give an interval errors. 
There is no intervals detected errors when running the code.
Note: please run eris in command line inside the github file

Try downloading UV

::: {#847336d4 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# For the simulated data
# uv run eris -i data/contigs_shovill_simulated.gfa \
#     -d data/IS.mmi \
#     -o output/eris_output_simulated

# # For the complete genome assembly
# uv run eris -i ../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta \
#     -d ../data/IS.mmi -o ../result/eris_output_complete \
#     -q

# mkdir -p result/complete_genome && mv result/eris_output_complete* result/complete_genome/
```
:::


We succesfully run Eris for both the simulated data and the complete genome assembly. We can now investigate the output files.

The problem with the complete genome assembly (from Dimas):
- Since it contains plasmids, ERIS didn't run successfully. The complete genome has less columns compared to the simulated data.

# Analysing the output files

::: {#ffb0eebe .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd
# Load the output files
simulated_df = pd.read_csv("../result/output_simulated/eris_output_simulated_report.tsv", sep="\t")
complete_df = pd.read_csv("../result/complete_genome/eris_output_complete_report.tsv", sep="\t")

# Check the dimension of each dataframe
print("Simulated data shape:", simulated_df.shape)
print("Complete genome assembly shape:", complete_df.shape)

# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in both datasets
simulated_counts = simulated_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
complete_counts = complete_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()

print("Total simulated data IS element counts:", simulated_counts.count())
print("Total complete genome assembly IS element counts:", complete_counts.count())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Simulated data shape: (293, 9)
Complete genome assembly shape: (37, 9)
Total simulated data IS element counts: 35
Total complete genome assembly IS element counts: 4
```
:::
:::


There are **only 4 IS found in the complete genome assembly**, while there are 35 distinct IS found in the simulated data. This means Eris doesn't perform well on the complete genome assembly, likely due to the presence of plasmids. We can investigate the IS elements found in both datasets by printing out the target column. 

::: {#f557e04b .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# In case we want to investigate the IS elements found in the complete genome assembly, we can print out the target column
print("IS elements found in complete genome assembly:")
print(complete_counts)

print("IS elements found in simulated data:")
print(simulated_counts)
```

--- 

# Analysis to improve the results of the complete genome assembly

After investigating the terminal, the complete genome assembly has .fasta format with only 3 long sequences. However, the simulated data has 456 fragmented sequences. 

::: {#9ab184f4 .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Check the number of sequences in the complete genome assembly
print("Number of sequences in complete genome assembly:")
!grep -c "^>" ../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta

# Check the number of sequences in the simulated data
print("Number of sequences in simulated data:")
!grep -c "^S" ../../data/contigs_shovill_simulated.gfa
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Number of sequences in complete genome assembly:
3
Number of sequences in simulated data:
456
```
:::
:::


## Convert the Fasta into GFA format
Since the complete genome assembly is in .fasta format, we need to convert it into .gfa format to run Eris. The .gfa format has an important information where we can see the overlaps, paths, and coverage, while the FASTA lack of this information. 

We used minigraph to convert the fasta file into gfa format. The command is as follows:

```{bash}
# minigraph -xggs data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta > data/EC958_complete_genome.gfa

# rerun Eris with the new gfa file
# uv run eris -i ../data/EC958_complete_genome.gfa -d ../data/IS.mmi -o ../result/complete_gfa/eris_complete_gfa
```

Weakness of this might be not real topological information of the genome assembly, as the gfa file is generated from the fasta file.

::: {#2edb9461 .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Load the new output file
complete_gfa_df = pd.read_csv("../result/complete_gfa/eris_complete_gfa_report.tsv", sep="\t")

# Check the dimension of the new dataframe
print("Dimension of the new dataframe:", complete_gfa_df.shape)
# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe
complete_gfa_counts = complete_gfa_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
print("Total IS element counts in the new dataframe:", complete_gfa_counts.count())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Dimension of the new dataframe: (37, 9)
Total IS element counts in the new dataframe: 4
```
:::
:::


Turns out that there are still only 4 IS found in the complete genome assembly after converting it into gfa format. 

## Fragmentation of the complete genome assembly from the fasta file
I want to try create a fragmentations of the complete genome assembly to see if it can improve the results. As the complete genome assembly has 3 sequences, I will try to create 150 fragments for each sequence.

::: {#ddb5ae6f .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Create fragments of the complete genome assembly
from Bio import SeqIO
from Bio.SeqRecord import SeqRecord
import random

complete_fasta = "../../data/EC958_complete_genome.fasta"
fragmented_fasta = "../../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta"

def fragment_genome(input_fasta: str, output_fasta: str, num_fragments: int = 150):
    records = list(SeqIO.parse(input_fasta, "fasta"))
    fragments = []
    
    for record in records:
        seq_length = len(record.seq)
        fragment_size = seq_length // num_fragments
        
        for i in range(num_fragments):
            start = i * fragment_size
            end = (i + 1) * fragment_size if i < num_fragments - 1 else seq_length
            fragment_seq = record.seq[start:end]
            fragment_id = f"{record.id}_fragment_{i+1}"
            fragments.append(SeqRecord(fragment_seq, id=fragment_id, description=""))
    
    SeqIO.write(fragments, output_fasta, "fasta")
    print(f"Written {len(fragments)} fragments to {output_fasta}")


# Fragment the complete genome assembly
fragment_genome(complete_fasta, 
    fragmented_fasta, 
    num_fragments=150)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Written 450 fragments to ../../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta
```
:::
:::


After creating the fragmented fasta file, we can convert it into gfa format and run Eris again to see if there are more IS elements found in the complete genome assembly.

```{bash}
# Try to use Eris with the fragmented complete genome assembly
# uv run eris --genome ../data/EC958_complete_genome_fragmented.fasta -d ../data/IS.mmi -o ../result/complete_fragmented/eris_complete_fragmented -q
```

### Investigate the output file after fragmentation

::: {#fb49900b .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Load the new output file after fragmentation
import pandas as pd
complete_fragmented_df = pd.read_csv("../result/complete_fragmented/eris_complete_fragmented_report.tsv", sep="\t")
# Check the dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation  
print("Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_df.shape)
# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe
complete_fragmented_counts = complete_fragmented_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
print("Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_counts.count())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation: (212, 9)
Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation: 24
```
:::
:::


There are more IS found in the fragmented genome. However, we want to know whether is it the same IS found in the simulated data. Otherwise also report, the one that not found in the simulated data.

::: {#b376b63a .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Check if the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly are also found in the simulated
common_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) & set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of common IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:", len(common_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:", common_fragmented)

# Check the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data
unique_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) - set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:", len(unique_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:", unique_fragmented)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
